title: Hydrogen-enhanced microbanding in an austenitic FeMnAlC low-density steel: Effect on hydrogen embrittlement resistance
abstract_summary: The influence of 101 mass ppm hydrogen on the room temperature deformation structure and mechanical behavior of an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel was investigated using ECCI, EBSD, and STEM. The steel exhibits high hydrogen embrittlement resistance with a moderate increase in yield stress (10%) and elongation to fracture (~8%). Hydrogen promotes hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB) and hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB), which are attributed to hydrogen-induced effects on dislocation plasticity. HEMB contributes to strain-hardening capacity and damage mitigation by enhancing plastic relaxation and geometrically necessary dislocation storage in sub-micron strain gradients.
research_objective: To elucidate the effect of hydrogen on dislocation structures and deformation mechanisms in an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel and to establish the role of hydrogen-induced microbanding in the steel's hydrogen embrittlement resistance.
key_sections: 3.2 Influence of hydrogen on the dislocation structure
4.1 Influence of hydrogen on the mechanical behavior
4.2 Influence of hydrogen on the dislocation structure
4.3 The effect of HEMB on HE resistance
4.4 Comparative analysis of the HE resistance of austenitic steels
